next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Marcia had spent the money so many times in her head that by Saturday afternoon it already felt warm from handling. First and security on a place with a real front door, not a converted garage somebody else could decide to call illegal whenever it suited them. The college money she had not named to Tiana because hope got loud in that apartment, and loud hope turned into appetite. The light bill. The little late fees that bred in the dark when you let them. The numbers changed depending on which office wanted blood first, but the total never moved. Five thousand eight hundred dollars. Enough to turn July from a cliff into chores.

Inside her handbag she carried three white envelopes with her block printing in the corners: RENT, SCHOOL, LIGHT. She had folded them flat before leaving, the same way she folded medication schedules for Celia Alvarez, crisp enough that even bad news had to stand up straight. Writing the labels ahead of time felt half like discipline and half like temptation. She had done it anyway. A woman could not wait on miracle and then pretend she had no use for it once it came.

People with checking accounts liked to talk about partner as if it were a quaint old-country habit, something to mention beside rum cake and airport barrels. Marcia never corrected them. Let them keep their little smile. A hand was not folklore. A hand was what people built when the bank charged thirty-five dollars because a paycheck landed one day wrong, when credit asked for paper the poor could not spare, when cash disappeared too easily inside a marriage or a family...
open_hooks_to_carry: Other families' ease sharpens the class gap without caricature.
The paperwork problem is concrete and procedural.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...tion from the kitchen in Coral Gables change shape. There was no use carrying it home as injury. Injury did not budget. Injury did not keep a roof on. By the time the bus rattled past a laundromat with half its letters gone, she had turned the whole morning into assignments.

Tomorrow she would give Yvette the full amount due, before Delroy could start talking around it.

If Delroy brought in cash this week, part of it would go straight into the next envelope before rice, before phone, before any bill that could be delayed without a man appearing at the door.

She would not tell the children how close the math sat to breaking yet. Fear made young people stupid in different directions. Tiana got colder. Micah got louder. Neither one of them needed to see how much of the summer was balanced on women passing envelopes after church and pretending that trust was stronger than misfortune.

The partner notebook stayed in her hand for the rest of the ride. She did not grip it. She held it. She ran her thumb over the cardboard cover until the edge softened with sweat. By the time the bus turned toward Little River, the cold air had gone thin and useless. People smelled like outside again: salt, old fryer grease, damp cotton, somebody's cheap coconut lotion giving up under the heat.

Marcia slipped the notebook back into her bag as carefully as if it were breakable.

July, she thought, was not a promise. It was a door, thin wood with a cheap lock, maybe swollen from rain already. But it was a door. Until it failed, every other bill in the house would have to wait its turn outside it.
state_deltas_required: Turn college from fantasy into fact by finishing orientation and aid paperwork.
Tiana's scholarship cannot be finalized, and she goes home ready for confrontation.
